# Conventions

Working agreements for this repository.

## Generated Files — Do Not Edit

The following files are generated by tooling and must never be edited by hand:

- `src/routeTree.gen.ts` — generated by TanStack Router (auto-updates on route file changes)
- `src/web/graphql/generated.ts` — generated by `npm run graphql:generate` (client types, `GqlC*` prefix)
- `src/server/graphql/generated.ts` — generated by `npm run graphql:generate` (server types, `GqlS*` prefix)
- `drizzle/` — generated by `npm run db:generate` (migration files)

To regenerate GraphQL types after a schema change:

```bash
npm run graphql:generate
```

## Database Schema (Drizzle)

### Adding a new table

1. Define the table in `src/server/db/schema.ts` using `pgTable()`
2. Export the inferred types: `type Foo = typeof foo.$inferSelect` and `type FooCreate = typeof foo.$inferInsert`
3. Generate a migration: `npm run db:generate`
4. Apply the migration: `npm run db:migrate`
5. For quick iteration during development, `npm run db:push` pushes schema changes directly without generating a migration file

### Adding a column to an existing table

Same workflow as above — modify the table definition in `schema.ts`, then generate and apply the migration.

## Icons

Use [Lucide React](https://lucide.dev/icons/) for all icons. It is already installed as `lucide-react`.

Always import the **Icon-suffixed** variant. This is enforced by ESLint (`no-restricted-imports`).

```tsx
// Good
import { ChevronRightIcon } from 'lucide-react';

// Bad — will fail lint
import { ChevronRight } from 'lucide-react';
```

## Naming Strategy

This project uses **entity-action naming** where file and function names lead with the entity (or domain concept) followed by the action or
role:

| Category  | Pattern              | Examples                                        |
| --------- | -------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- |
| Commands  | `{entity}{Action}`   | `sessionUpsert`, `userCreate`                   |
| Queries   | `{entity}{Query}`    | `userFindOne`, `sessionFindMany`                |
| Mappers   | `toGql{Entity}`      | `toGqlSession`, `toGqlUser`                     |
| Guards    | `guard{Entity}{Ctx}` | `guardUserSubscription`, `guardSessionMutation` |
| Factories | `{entity}Create`     | `serverRuntimeCreate`, `resolversCreate`        |
| Utils     | `{entity}Utils`      | `sessionUtils`                                  |
| Tests     | `{source}.test`      | `sessionUtils.test`, `sessionUpsert.test`       |
| Types     | `PascalCase`         | `ServerRuntime`, `PubSubPostgres`               |

This keeps related files sorted together in directory listings (all `session`\* files appear next to each other).

## Comments

Only add a comment if there is no other way to make the code self-explanatory. Reach for the alternatives first:

- A clearer name for the variable, function, or type
- A smaller, well-named helper function that captures the intent
- A more precise type that encodes the constraint
- Restructured control flow that removes the surprise

Comments are reserved for things the code itself cannot express: external context (a spec, a bug, a workaround), non-obvious **why**
decisions, or load-bearing invariants that aren't visible at the call site. The Arrange / Act / Assert markers in tests and the two-phase
markers in commands are intentional exceptions — they're structural, not explanatory.

If a comment paraphrases what the next line does, delete the comment and fix the line instead.

## UI Components

- Base components live in `src/web/components/base/` — these are Radix UI / shadcn primitives. Modify them sparingly.
- Application-specific components live directly in `src/web/components/`.
- Use the `cn()` utility from `src/web/utils/cn.ts` for conditional class merging.

## Brand Colors and Fonts

The site has a small, opinionated brand palette and a three-font system. Tokens live in `src/styles.css` and are exposed as Tailwind
utilities (`bg-aubergine`, `text-cream`, `font-serif`, `font-mono`, …). Every page reaches into the same tokens — never re-introduce raw hex
values like `bg-[#FBF7F3]` once a token exists for that role. Reusable visual patterns (pill buttons, section eyebrow + rule, service cards,
credential blocks) are documented in `docs/style/patterns.md`.

- Palette and when-to-use rules: [`docs/style/colors.md`](./style/colors.md)
- Type system and per-face rules: [`docs/style/typography.md`](./style/typography.md)
- Reusable patterns: [`docs/style/patterns.md`](./style/patterns.md)

## GraphQL

- The schema is defined SDL-first in `src/server/graphql/schema.graphqls`.
- After any schema change, run `npm run graphql:generate` to regenerate types.
- Server-side generated types use the `GqlS` prefix; client-side types use `GqlC`.
- All resolvers are wired in `src/server/graphql/resolversCreate.ts` — this is the only file that should import from commands/, queries/,
  and guards/.
- Union and interface `__resolveType` declarations go in `src/server/graphql/extensions.ts` via module augmentation.
- Client-side `.graphql` operation files go alongside their route or component: `src/routes/**/*.graphql` or
  `src/web/components/**/*.graphql`.

## Commands (Database Writes)

Commands in `src/server/commands/` follow a two-phase structure: **payload construction**, then **transactional execution**.

### Structure

```typescript
export async function orderCreate(db: Database, input: OrderInput): Promise<GqlSOrder> {
  // Phase 1 — Payload construction (pure, no DB calls)
  const orderInsert: OrderCreate = {
    orderId: crypto.randomUUID(),
    customerId: input.customerId,
    status: 'pending',
  };

  const orderItemInserts: OrderItemCreate[] = input.items.map((item) => ({
    orderItemId: crypto.randomUUID(),
    orderId: orderInsert.orderId,
    productId: item.productId,
    quantity: item.quantity,
  }));

  // Phase 2 — Transactional execution (side-effectful)
  try {
    const order = await db.transaction(async (transaction) => {
      const [created] = await transaction.insert(orders).values(orderInsert).returning();
      await transaction.insert(orderItems).values(orderItemInserts);
      return created!;
    });

    return toGqlOrder(order);
  } catch (error) {
    console.error(error);
    throw error;
  }
}
```

### Rules

1. **Phase 1 — Payload construction** comes first. Build all insert/update objects before touching the database. This phase is pure: no DB
   calls, no side effects. If it fails (e.g. a Zod parse), no connection or transaction is ever opened.
2. **Phase 2 — Transactional execution** wraps all DB calls in `db.transaction(async (transaction) => { ... })`. Use `transaction` (not
   `db`) for every query inside the transaction. Wrap in try-catch, log the error, and re-throw.
3. **Single-write commands** do not need a transaction — individual statements are already atomic in PostgreSQL. Use the two-phase structure
   (payload, then single DB call in try-catch) but skip `db.transaction()`.
4. **The `DatabaseTransaction` type** is exported from `src/server/db/index.ts`. Use it when passing a transaction handle to helper
   functions.

## Package Manager

npm only. The `engines` field in `package.json` enforces this — pnpm and yarn are blocked.

## German Copy — Gender-Neutral Language

All German user-facing copy in this site uses gender-neutral language. The generic masculine (e.g. _einen Podologen_, _Patienten_,
_Diabetiker_, _Mitarbeiter_) is not used to refer to mixed or unspecified groups.

When writing or editing German strings (route files, SEO descriptions, alt text, agent prompts, anything a visitor reads), pick the first
option from this list that fits — earlier options are preferred because they remove the question entirely:

1. **Rephrase to an abstract noun or activity.** Almost always possible. The cleanest fix and the one to reach for first.
   - _Brauche ich einen Podologen?_ → _Brauche ich eine podologische Behandlung?_
   - _Zeit pro Patient_ → _Zeit pro Behandlung_
   - _zwischen jedem Patienten_ → _zwischen jeder Behandlung_
   - _Sicheres Arbeiten mit Diabetikern_ → _Sicheres Arbeiten bei Diabetes / beim diabetischen Fußsyndrom_
   - _viele Stammkunden_ → _eine gewachsene Stammkundschaft_
2. **Use the paired form** (_Patientinnen und Patienten_, _Podologinnen und Podologen_) when a personal noun cannot be avoided. Always full
   words — no Binnen-I, no asterisk, no colon, no slash inside running prose. The site's voice is calm and editorial; pair forms read
   cleanly there.
3. **Use _(m/w/d)_** only in formal job-posting headings where it is the legally established convention (e.g. _Podologin / Podologe
   (m/w/d)_). Do not sprinkle it through body copy.

Annette is a single named person — referring to _her_ specifically as _Podologin_ / _Heilpraktikerin_ is correct and stays. The rule is
about generic references to groups or roles, not about the practice owner.

The English copy follows the standard inclusive-language conventions of the language (e.g. _podiatrist_, _patient_) — it does not need a
paired form because English nouns aren't grammatically gendered.

## SEO

Every public page route MUST set `head: ({ params }) => seoMeta({ ... })` using the helper at `src/web/seo/seoMeta.ts`. Pages that are
logged-in, transactional, or otherwise non-indexable pass `noindex: true`. Indexable public pages are also added to `SITEMAP_PATHS` in
`src/web/seo/sitemapRoutes.ts` so they appear in the dynamic `/sitemap.xml`.

See [architecture/seo.md](./architecture/seo.md) for the full rationale, the canonical/hreflang strategy, and a copy-paste template for new
pages.

## Environment Variables

Never read `process.env` directly. The only sanctioned reader is `src/server/env/environmentVariablesCreate.ts`; everything else (including
`drizzle.config.ts` and test utilities) calls `environmentVariablesCreate()` to get a typed, validated object. This is enforced by an ESLint
`no-restricted-syntax` rule — any new `process.env` reference will fail lint. See
`[docs/architecture/environment.md](./architecture/environment.md)` for the rationale.

## Testing

### Runner

Tests use [Vitest](https://vitest.dev/). Run the full suite with:

```bash
npm test
```

### Configuration — two projects

Vitest config lives in **`vitest.config.ts`**, separate from `vite.config.ts`. The dev/build config loads the full TanStack Start + Nitro +
React plugin stack, which Vitest 4 + Vite 8's module runner cannot evaluate against React's CJS entry — keeping it loaded during tests
produces a `ReferenceError: module is not defined` at startup.

`vitest.config.ts` defines two projects with disjoint plugin sets:

| Project  | Environment | Plugins                  | Includes                                                      |
| -------- | ----------- | ------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `server` | `node`      | none                     | `src/server/**/*.test.{ts,tsx}`                               |
| `web`    | `jsdom`     | `@vitejs/plugin-react()` | `src/web/**/*.test.{ts,tsx}`, `src/routes/**/*.test.{ts,tsx}` |

Run a single project with `npm test -- --project=web` (or `--project=server`).

### Setup files

- `src/server/test/vitestSetup.ts` runs in **both** projects. It globally spies on `console.log/error/warn/info/debug` with a no-op so the
  logger's stdout echo doesn't pollute reporter output, and so any test can assert on calls via
  `expect(console.error).toHaveBeenCalledWith(...)` without per-file `vi.spyOn` boilerplate.
- `src/web/test/vitestSetup.ts` runs only in the `web` project. It calls `cleanup()` from `@testing-library/react` after each test so
  `document.body` is reset between renders.

`clearMocks: true` is set at the top level so it applies to both projects — `vi.fn()` call history resets between tests automatically.

### Structure — Arrange / Act / Assert

Every test case follows the **Arrange → Act → Assert** pattern with explicit comments:

```typescript
it('creates a new session when no existing session ID is provided', async () => {
  // Arrange
  const input = buildInput();

  // Act
  const result = await sessionUpsert(testDb, testLog, input);

  // Assert
  expect(result.sessionId).toBeDefined();
});
```

Use `// Arrange — nothing` when the test needs no setup.

### Unit tests

Place test files next to the module they test with a `.test.ts` (or `.test.tsx` for React) suffix:

```
src/server/utils/sessionUtils.ts
src/server/utils/sessionUtils.test.ts

src/web/components/base/button.tsx
src/web/components/base/button.test.tsx
```

Server unit tests cover pure or near-pure functions and should not touch the database. Web unit tests use `@testing-library/react`
(`render`, `screen`) — see `src/web/components/base/button.test.tsx` for the canonical shape.

### Integration tests (database)

Tests that hit the database use the shared test utilities in `src/server/test/commandTestUtils.ts`:

| Export                    | Purpose                                                                                                     |
| ------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `testDb`                  | Drizzle client connected to the test DB                                                                     |
| `testLog`                 | Logger wired to the test DB                                                                                 |
| `commandSetup()`          | Persists a fresh session row + a fresh `ServerRuntime` stub; returns `{ serverRuntime, requestingSession }` |
| `commandSetup.withUser()` | Same, plus a seeded `users` row; returns `{ serverRuntime, requestingSession, user }`                       |
| `findLogsForSession`      | Drains the async logger queue then returns the `Logs` rows for a `sessionId`                                |

**Always use a real database** — never mock Drizzle or the database layer. Each `commandSetup()` call mints fresh ids
(`crypto.randomUUID()`), so tests do not collide and there is no need for cleanup hooks between them. `vitest.config.ts` sets
`clearMocks: true`, which resets `vi.fn()` call history between tests, so individual tests do not need a `beforeEach(vi.clearAllMocks)` of
their own.

The canonical command test (see `src/server/commands/userUpdate.test.ts`) follows this shape — no `beforeAll`, no `beforeEach`, no shared
runtime; each `it` arranges what it needs:

```typescript
describe('userUpdate', () => {
  it('updates the row and publishes a userUpdates event on success', async () => {
    // Arrange
    const { serverRuntime, requestingSession, user } = await commandSetup.withUser({ name: 'original' });

    // Act
    const result = await userUpdate(user.userId, { user: { name: 'renamed' } }, requestingSession, serverRuntime);

    // Assert
    expect(result).toEqual({ success: true });
    expect(serverRuntime.publish.userUpdates).toHaveBeenCalledWith({ userId: user.userId });
  });
});
```

Environment variables for integration tests come from the shell environment. Unit tests must not depend on `.env` files — pass any required
config explicitly (e.g. inject a source object into `environmentVariablesCreate`).

## Commit Messages

Commits follow [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) and are linted by [commitlint](https://commitlint.js.org/) using
`@commitlint/config-conventional` (see `commitlint.config.ts`).

```
<type>(<optional scope>): <subject>
```

Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `docs`, `refactor`, `test`, `chore`, `perf`, `build`, `ci`, `style`, `revert`.

```bash
# Good
feat(chat): stream agent responses over SSE
fix: guard against missing session cookie

# Bad — will fail commitlint
updated stuff
WIP
```

Enforcement happens in two places:

- A Husky `commit-msg` hook (`.husky/commit-msg`) runs `commitlint --edit $1` locally on every commit. `npm run prepare` (executed
  automatically on `npm install`) installs the hook.
- `npm run commitlint:check` lints the most recent commit (`HEAD~1..HEAD`) and is part of `npm run check`.

## Quality Checks

Run all checks before pushing:

```bash
npm run check
```

This runs: format check, lint, spell check, type check, unused export detection (knip), and commitlint on the last commit.

## Server-Side Rendering (Playwright)

The template ships a server-side image-rendering pipeline. To use it:

- **Never `import 'playwright'` directly.** Capture is exposed on the runtime as `serverRuntime.browser.capture(options)`. Tests inject a
  stub.
- **Render targets live under `/server/*`** (e.g. `src/routes/server.reports.$reportId.tsx`). They authenticate with a one-shot HMAC token
  from `src/server/utils/serverToken.ts`, not the session cookie.
- **The signing secret is `SERVER_TOKEN_SECRET`** (capability-specific — only required by features that actually call `browserCapture`). Set
  it in `.env.local` and in your Coolify environment.
- **Do not edit `vite.config.ts`'s Playwright externals or the runtime stage of `Dockerfile`** without reading
  [architecture/server-side-rendering.md](./architecture/server-side-rendering.md) — `playwright` must stay external on both, and the
  runtime image must keep installing Chromium.

## Jobs (Background Processing)

- Job handler files live in `src/server/jobs/handlers/` and follow entity-action naming: `{entityAction}.ts` (e.g.,
  `staleSessionsCleanup.ts`, `signupReminderSend.ts`)
- Each handler file exports a single `JobDefinition` (either `RecurringJobDefinition` or `QueuedJobDefinition`)
- All jobs are registered in `src/server/jobs/jobDefinitions.ts` — this is the single wiring point (analogous to `resolversCreate.ts` for
  GraphQL)
- Enqueue queued jobs via `serverRuntime.jobs.enqueue(definition, data, options)` — the definition provides type safety
- Re-export handler definitions from `src/server/jobs/index.ts` for ergonomic imports
- pg-boss manages its own `pgboss` schema in PostgreSQL — do not create Drizzle migrations for it
